title: The newest entrant in the military's launch competition isn't actually a launch company
gist: The US Space Force added in-space propulsion firm Impulse Space (alongside Relativity Space) to its NSSL Phase 3 Lane 1 launch pool, an unusual move driven by unmet demand for high-energy GEO launch that Impulse's Helios kick stage can serve.

themes.theme: A new category in national security launch

insights.claim: The Space Force added Impulse Space, an in-space propulsion company rather than a launch provider, to its Lane 1 bidder pool, a move even Impulse's president says Phase 3 rules 'did not contemplate.'
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Regulatory flexibility to admit a non-launch firm signals the military values high-energy orbital delivery capability over vehicle ownership, widening who can compete.
insights.quote: I think it's fair to say that Phase 3 did not contemplate this
insights.locator: The addition of Impulse Space, however, was something of a surprise

insights.claim: Impulse's Helios kick stage provides up to 9 km/s of delta-V, transforming a medium-lift rocket like Falcon 9 into one with the performance to rapidly boost payloads from low-Earth to geostationary orbit ~36,000 km up.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: A kick stage that upgrades cheaper rockets to GEO capability could reshape launch economics for high-energy orbits, a competitive lever against heavy-lift vehicles.
insights.locator: Impulse Space is developing a kick stage it calls Helios

insights.claim: Helios is compatible with a range of rockets from Falcon 9 to boosters from ULA, Rocket Lab, and Relativity Space, and Impulse would provide end-to-end service procuring the launch and stacking payloads.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: Rocket-agnostic compatibility positions Impulse as a horizontal layer across the launch market rather than tied to one vehicle.

insights.claim: NSSL Phase 3 Lane 1 targets newer, more risk-tolerant missions with about 30 launches set aside worth roughly $5.6 billion, while Lane 2 reserves the most valuable payloads for SpaceX and ULA.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: The $5.6B Lane 1 pool quantifies the revenue opportunity drawing new entrants into military launch.

insights.claim: Impulse must first demonstrate Helios in flight (targeted for a 2027 debut on a Falcon 9) before it can win task orders, with launches following 18-24 months after.
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: The 2027 flight demo is the key de-risking milestone gating Impulse's access to military revenue.
insights.locator: To be eligible to bid on Lane 1 contracts

insights.claim: Relativity Space, led by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, was added to Lane 1 on credible progress toward its partly reusable heavy-lift Terran R rocket, joining prior additions Rocket Lab (Neutron) and Stoke Space (Nova).
insights.speaker: Eric Berger
insights.so_what: The widening Lane 1 field intensifies competition below SpaceX/ULA, diversifying the military's launch supplier base.
